summary:
Saladin (Salah ad-Din Yusuf ibn Ayyub, 1137–1193 AD) was the founder of the Ayyubid Dynasty and the greatest Muslim military and political leader of the Crusading era, who reunified Egypt, Syria, and parts of Mesopotamia under a single authority and recaptured Jerusalem from the [[event:92|Crusaders]] in 1187 — reversing the [[event:2221|capture of 1099]] and rendering the [[event:2222|Third Crusade]] unable to reclaim the holy city. A Kurdish Muslim of remarkable personal charisma and genuine piety, Saladin rose from a military officer in the Zengid court to the self-appointed sultan of Egypt (deposing the Shia Fatimid Caliph in 1171) and eventually the dominant power of the Near East.

Saladin's reputation transcended religious boundaries. His conduct at the [[event:2274|Battle of Hattin]] and capture of Jerusalem — where he explicitly forbade the massacre of the defeated Christian population, in deliberate contrast to the 1099 Crusader massacre — was celebrated across the medieval world, including by Christian writers, as an example of chivalric conduct. His personal meeting with [[event:3535|Richard I of England]] during the Third Crusade generated a romantic mythology of courtly respect between honorable enemies. In the Islamic world, Saladin became the symbol of Muslim resistance to Crusader occupation and an enduring hero of Sunni Islam. The Ayyubid Dynasty he founded governed Egypt and Syria until the mid-13th century, when it was replaced by the [[event:2275|Mamluk Sultanate]] — the state that would deliver the decisive blow against the Crusaders at the [[event:2978|Battle of Ain Jalut]] and eventually expel them from the Levant entirely.
